phase.title: Stored Credentials & Files
phase.summary: Hunt for plaintext or recoverable credentials on disk: Unattend.xml, SAM backups, IIS config, GPP, PowerShell history, DPAPI, Credential Manager.

nodes.title: Unattend.xml / Autounattend.xml / sysprep.inf
nodes.summary: Imaging/provisioning artefacts that often retain the local-admin password (sometimes base64 encoded).
nodes.description_md:
Windows deployment tools (WDS, SCCM, MDT, sysprep) use XML answer files that can contain credentials for automating the setup process. These files often persist long after deployment and are world-readable.

**Common file locations**:
- `C:\Windows\Panther\Unattend.xml`
- `C:\Windows\Panther\Unattended.xml`
- `C:\Windows\System32\sysprep\sysprep.xml`
- `C:\Windows\System32\sysprep\sysprep.inf`
- `C:\unattend.xml`
- `C:\autounattend.xml`

**Password encoding**: Passwords in unattend files may be in plaintext (`<Value>Password123</Value>`) or base64 encoded. For base64, they are NOT encrypted — just `[System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('<b64>'))` decodes them.

**Common fields containing creds**: `<Password>`, `<AdministratorPassword>`, `<DomainAdminPassword>`, autologon credentials.
nodes.detection_hints: Reading Unattend.xml from non-SYSTEM processes; WinPEAS/winpeas `filesinfo` module findings; Sysmon file access events for sysprep.xml.

nodes.title: SAM / SYSTEM hive backups
nodes.summary: Legacy and recovery installs leave SAM + SYSTEM copies in C:\Windows\Repair\ or shadow copies.
nodes.description_md:
The SAM (Security Account Manager) database stores NTLM hashes for local accounts, protected by the SYSTEM key stored in the SYSTEM hive. Backup copies often have weaker ACLs than the live hive.

**Common locations for backup copies**:
- `C:\Windows\Repair\SAM` and `C:\Windows\Repair\SYSTEM`
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\SAM` and `...\SYSTEM`
- Volume Shadow Copies: `\\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\Windows\System32\config\SAM`

**Extraction workflow** (attacker host):
```bash
secretsdump.py -sam SAM -system SYSTEM LOCAL
```
This yields NTLM hashes of all local accounts (Administrator, Guest, custom accounts). Use `pass-the-hash` or crack offline with Hashcat.

**Note**: Shadow copy SAM backups are readable without admin via HiveNightmare/SeriousSAM (CVE-2021-36934) on unpatched systems.
nodes.detection_hints: Reads to `C:\Windows\Repair\` or `C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\` by non-SYSTEM processes; VSS shadow copy access events.

nodes.title: IIS web.config / applicationHost.config
nodes.summary: Connection strings often contain plaintext SQL credentials and application-pool identities.
nodes.description_md:
IIS web applications store database connection strings and application settings in `web.config` files. These frequently contain plaintext SQL Server credentials (`Data Source=;User ID=;Password=`) or Windows identity credentials for service accounts.

**Higher-value target**: `C:\Windows\System32\inetsrv\config\applicationHost.config` contains virtual directory passwords and application pool identity credentials — potentially more sensitive than individual app web.config files.

**Key strings to search for**:
- `connectionString` / `connectionStrings`
- `password` / `pwd` / `pass`
- `userName` / `uid`
- `DefaultCredentials`

**Impersonation credentials**: App pools running as domain accounts have their credentials in applicationHost.config — these are often domain users with server access.
nodes.detection_hints: Read access to web.config or applicationHost.config by non-IIS, non-SYSTEM processes; grep for credential patterns in IIS config directories.

nodes.title: McAfee SiteList.xml
nodes.summary: Legacy McAfee installs leave SiteList.xml with a recoverable encrypted password.
nodes.description_md:
Legacy McAfee ePO (ePolicy Orchestrator) deployments create a `SiteList.xml` file that stores the credentials for the McAfee update repository. The password is encrypted with a well-known static XOR key, making decryption trivial.

**File locations**:
- `C:\ProgramData\SiteList.xml`
- `C:\Program Files (x86)\McAfee\Common Framework\SiteList.xml`
- `C:\Documents and Settings\All Users\Application Data\McAfee\Common Framework\SiteList.xml`

**Decryption**: The [mcafee-sitelist-pwd-decryption](https://github.com/funoverip/mcafee-sitelist-pwd-decryption) tool decrypts the password using the hardcoded key. Recovered credentials are often reused for domain accounts or SMB shares.

**Prevalence**: McAfee was widely deployed in enterprise environments. Even on systems that have migrated to other AV, the legacy SiteList.xml may remain.
nodes.detection_hints: Access to SiteList.xml by non-McAfee processes; file presence in standard McAfee install locations as an indicator of vulnerable credential storage.

nodes.title: Group Policy Preferences cPassword (SYSVOL)
nodes.summary: Classic AES-encrypted password on SYSVOL with a published key. Affects pre-MS14-025 environments.
nodes.description_md:
Group Policy Preferences (GPP) allowed administrators to set local account passwords, map drives, and schedule tasks using credentials stored in policy XML files on SYSVOL. Microsoft hardcoded an AES-256 encryption key into the Group Policy client (to allow all domain members to decrypt the policies), and [published that key in MSDN documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/openspecs/windows_protocols/ms-gppref/2c15cbf0-f086-4c74-8b70-1f2fa45dd4be?redirectedfrom=MSDN).

**Result**: Any domain user can read SYSVOL and decrypt the `cpassword` field with the published key.

**MS14-025** (2014) removed the ability to create *new* GPP password entries but did not remove existing ones. Environments not cleaned up since 2014 remain vulnerable.

**Target files**: `Groups.xml`, `Services.xml`, `Scheduledtasks.xml`, `Datasources.xml`, `Printers.xml` under `\\<domain>\SYSVOL\<domain>\Policies\`.

**Decryption**: `gpp-decrypt <base64-cpassword>` (included in Kali).
nodes.detection_hints: Domain member accessing SYSVOL policy XML files and immediately using the recovered credential; MS14-025 detection should be routine — any cpassword still present in SYSVOL is a finding.

nodes.title: PowerShell history (ConsoleHost_history.txt)
nodes.summary: Past commands often contain admins running net use with plaintext passwords or API tokens.
nodes.description_md:
PowerShell PSReadLine saves a persistent command history file at `%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt`. Administrators frequently type `net use`, `runas`, API calls, or script invocations that include plaintext credentials in the history.

**Common credential patterns**:
- `net use \\server\share /user:DOMAIN\user Password123`
- `$cred = New-Object PSCredential('admin', ('P@ssw0rd' | ConvertTo-SecureString -AsPlainText -Force))`
- Database connection strings in scripts
- SSH/SCP commands with embedded passwords

**Access**: Each user's history is in their own `%APPDATA%` path. With admin or SeBackupPrivilege, read other users' history files. The PowerShell command to read all users' histories loops through `C:\Users\*\`.
nodes.detection_hints: Sensitive patterns (`password`, `cred`, `/user:`) appearing in PowerShell history file reads; anomalous access to other users' AppData directories.

nodes.title: Stored RDP credentials
nodes.summary: .rdp files with `password 51:b:` are DPAPI-encrypted blobs recoverable per-user.
nodes.description_md:
Remote Desktop Connection (`.rdp` files) can embed saved credentials. The `password 51:b:` field contains a DPAPI-encrypted blob specific to the creating user's DPAPI master key. Recovery is possible only if you have that user's session or their master key.

**Discovery**: `.rdp` files often live on the desktop or in Documents — users save them for convenience.

**Recovery options**:
1. **In-context**: Running as the user, use Mimikatz `dpapi::rdg /in:<blob>` or cmdkey/vault tools
2. **With master key**: `dpapi::rdg /in:<blob> /masterkey:<mk>` in Mimikatz
3. **Via DPAPI with domain backup key**: If you've DCSync'd the DPAPI domain backup key (`dpapi::backupkeys /system:<dc>`), decrypt any user's DPAPI blobs

**Credential Manager**: Saved RDP credentials may also appear under Windows Credential Manager (`cmdkey /list`) as `TERMSRV/hostname` entries.
nodes.detection_hints: Access to `.rdp` files outside normal user paths; DPAPI decryption events; Credential Manager reads of TERMSRV entries.

nodes.title: Credential Manager
nodes.summary: List stored generic + domain credentials; many can be reused via runas /savecred.
nodes.description_md:
Windows Credential Manager (`cmdkey`) stores network credentials (domain logins, web credentials, certificate-based) that Windows automatically presents when connecting to the target resource. Stored credentials listed as `runas /savecred` targets can be reused without knowing the actual password.

**Key findings**:
- Domain\user credentials for servers where the current user has logged in
- `TERMSRV/hostname` entries — RDP saved passwords
- Generic credentials from applications

**Reuse without cracking**: `runas /user:DOMAIN\admin /savecred cmd.exe` — if the credential is stored, this spawns cmd as that user without prompting for a password.

**Mimikatz extraction**: `sekurlsa::credman` dumps Credential Manager in cleartext when LSASS is accessible (requires admin/SeDebug). The Windows Vault API can also enumerate and extract secrets in the user's context.
nodes.detection_hints: cmdkey.exe execution from scripts or shells; `runas /savecred` from non-interactive contexts; DPAPI decryption of Credential Manager blobs.

nodes.title: DPAPI master keys & blobs
nodes.summary: Decrypt browser saved logins, Credential Manager blobs, and saved RDP via DPAPI master key.
nodes.description_md:
DPAPI (Data Protection API) is used by Windows to encrypt user secrets (browser logins, RDP passwords, WiFi keys, Credential Manager entries) using a key derived from the user's password hash. Decryption is possible:

1. **In the user's session**: DPAPI decrypts transparently — tools like SharpDPAPI, Mimikatz, or LaZagne can extract all secrets without knowing the password
2. **With the user's master key**: Captured from LSASS (`sekurlsa::dpapi`) or from the user's profile
3. **With the domain DPAPI backup key**: DCSync recovers the domain backup key, enabling decryption of any user's DPAPI blobs offline

**Master key storage**: `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Protect\<SID>\<GUID>` — these are per-user, per-context master key blobs.

**Attack chain**: SeBackupPrivilege → copy DPAPI master key blobs → recover domain backup key via DCSync → decrypt all blobs offline.
nodes.detection_hints: Master key file reads from Protect\ directory; DPAPI API calls (`CryptProtectData`/`CryptUnprotectData`) outside normal application contexts; Mimikatz `dpapi::` module usage.

nodes.title: Mass file search (password / pwd / cred)
nodes.summary: Brute-force grep across drives for common credential strings.
nodes.description_md:
A broad filesystem grep for credential-pattern strings is often the fastest way to find custom application configs, developer-left secrets, and backup files containing passwords. Tools run by sysadmins frequently write credentials to log files, scripts, and config files without considering long-term security.

**High-value file types**: `.xml`, `.ini`, `.config`, `.json`, `.yml`, `.yaml`, `.txt`, `.ps1`, `.bat`, `.cmd`, `.vbs`, `.env`

**High-value directories**:
- `C:\Users\*\Desktop\`, `C:\Users\*\Documents\`
- `C:\inetpub\`
- `C:\ProgramData\`
- Script directories (`C:\Scripts\`, `C:\Temp\`, `C:\Tools\`)

**PowerShell one-liner** with context output:
```powershell
Select-String -Path C:\ -Pattern 'password|pwd=|secret=' -Include *.xml,*.ini,*.config,*.txt -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```

**Note**: Use `2>nul` / `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` to suppress permission errors. Focus on non-Windows paths first.
nodes.detection_hints: Mass file read operations (`Select-String` / `findstr`) across the C: drive by non-admin accounts; high volume of file open events in process access telemetry.

nodes.title: Saved Wi-Fi passwords
nodes.summary: netsh exports cleartext keys for stored wireless profiles.
nodes.description_md: Stored WLAN profiles keep the PSK recoverable in cleartext via `netsh`. Useful for lateral movement onto the same network or password reuse.

snippets.title: Dump all WLAN keys
snippets.code: for /f "skip=9 tokens=1,2 delims=:" %i in ('netsh wlan show profiles') do @echo %j | findstr -i -v echo | netsh wlan show profile "%j" key=clear

references.title: MITRE T1555 — Credentials from Password Stores

nodes.title: Windows Credential Vault
nodes.summary: Enumerate the Vault for stored web and Windows credentials.
nodes.description_md: The Credential Vault holds saved web logins and Windows credentials. `vaultcmd` lists them; tooling (e.g. SharpWeb/Mimikatz `vault::list`) can recover secrets for the current user.

nodes.title: Browser saved credentials
nodes.summary: Chrome/Edge/Firefox stores logins and cookies decryptable in-context.
nodes.description_md: Chromium browsers store logins in an SQLite `Login Data` DB encrypted with a DPAPI-protected key in `Local State`. Running as the user (or with their DPAPI master key) decrypts them — SharpChrome/LaZagne automate this.

references.title: MITRE T1555.003 — Credentials from Web Browsers

nodes.title: LaZagne — credential harvester
nodes.summary: One-shot recovery of creds from dozens of local applications.
nodes.description_md: **LaZagne** sweeps browsers, mail clients, Wi-Fi, databases, sysadmin tools, and more, decrypting locally-stored secrets for the current user. The fastest broad credential sweep on a foothold.

nodes.title: KeePass database & master key
nodes.summary: Find .kdbx files and abuse trigger/keylogging or KeeThief to extract entries.
nodes.description_md: KeePass `.kdbx` databases are common on engineering boxes. With the user's session, KeeThief can pull the master key from memory, or a malicious trigger config can export the DB. Always search for `.kdbx` first.

nodes.title: App config secrets (WinSCP, FileZilla, OpenVPN)
nodes.summary: Connection managers persist passwords in predictable config files/registry.
nodes.description_md: WinSCP stores sessions (sometimes with recoverable passwords) in the registry/`WinSCP.ini`; FileZilla keeps `sitemanager.xml`/`recentservers.xml` with base64 passwords; OpenVPN `auth-user-pass` files hold creds. Grep these first.

nodes.title: Cloud CLI credentials
nodes.summary: AWS/Azure/GCP CLI tokens on disk pivot from host to cloud.
nodes.description_md: Developer boxes frequently cache cloud creds: `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.azure/`, `gcloud` config. These tokens bridge a local foothold into the cloud control plane.

nodes.title: SSH private keys & known_hosts
nodes.summary: OpenSSH keys under .ssh enable lateral movement to Linux/network gear.
nodes.description_md: Windows OpenSSH stores keys in `%USERPROFILE%\.ssh`. Private keys (often unprotected) plus `known_hosts` reveal both the credential and the targets it unlocks.

snippets.title: Find SSH material
snippets.code: Get-ChildItem "$env:USERPROFILE\.ssh" -EA SilentlyContinue; Get-ChildItem C:\Users -Recurse -Include id_rsa,id_ed25519 -EA SilentlyContinue

nodes.title: Cleartext secrets across the registry
nodes.summary: Search the whole registry for password-like values.
nodes.description_md: Beyond Winlogon autologon, secrets hide in arbitrary application keys. A registry-wide search for `password` catches installer artifacts, service configs, and bespoke apps.

snippets.title: Grep registry for password
snippets.code:
reg query HKLM /f password /t REG_SZ /s
reg query HKCU /f password /t REG_SZ /s

nodes.title: LSASS memory dump
nodes.summary: With admin/SeDebug, dump LSASS to recover hashes and cleartext secrets offline.
nodes.description_md: LSASS holds logon secrets (NTLM hashes, sometimes cleartext, Kerberos keys). Dump it (comsvcs.dll MiniDump, Task Manager, or a tool) and parse offline with Mimikatz/pypykatz. Heavily monitored by EDR.
nodes.detection_hints: Handle opens to lsass.exe with PROCESS_VM_READ; comsvcs MiniDump invocation; new .dmp files.

snippets.title: comsvcs MiniDump
snippets.code: for /f "tokens=2" %i in ('tasklist ^| findstr /i lsass') do rundll32 C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll, MiniDump %i C:\Temp\lsass.dmp full
